### Post by civillian on 2008-12-16
After I installed 8.10 (fresh install from linux format cover-disc) and installing the nvidia drivers for my GeForce 8400 from ubuntu restricted driver manager (and then updating from proposed updates as suggested on another forum) I am stuck with a resolution of 1024x768, which is a step down from my monitors full resolution of 1280x1024 (the monitor is an LG Flatron L1718S)

My xorg.conf is as follows

```
# nvidia-settings: X configuration file generated by nvidia-settings
# nvidia-settings:  version 1.0  (buildd@palmer)  Mon Nov  3 08:46:46 UTC 2008

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Layout0"
    Screen      0  "Screen0" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Keyboard0" "CoreKeyboard"
    InputDevice    "Mouse0" "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load           "dbe"
    Load           "extmod"
    Load           "type1"
    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "glx"
EndSection

Section "ServerFlags"
    Option         "Xinerama" "0"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    # generated from default
    Identifier     "Mouse0"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "Protocol" "auto"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/psaux"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "no"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    # generated from default
    Identifier     "Keyboard0"
    Driver         "kbd"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    # HorizSync source: builtin, VertRefresh source: builtin
    Identifier     "Monitor0"
    VendorName     "Unknown"
    ModelName      "CRT-0"
    HorizSync       28.0 - 55.0
    VertRefresh     43.0 - 72.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Device0"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
    BoardName      "GeForce 8400 GS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Device0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "TwinView" "0"
    Option         "TwinViewXineramaInfoOrder" "CRT-0"
    Option         "metamodes" "1024x768_60 +0+0"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1280x1024_60" "1024x768_50"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

```

and the output of xrandr - is 

```
Screen 0: minimum 320 x 240, current 1024 x 768, maximum 1024 x 768
default connected 1024x768+0+0 0mm x 0mm
   1024x768       50.0* 
   800x600        51.0     52.0     53.0  
   680x384        54.0     55.0  
   640x480        56.0  
   512x384        57.0  
   400x300        58.0  
   320x240        59.0 
```

Any suggestions would be welcome!

---

### Post by XsCode on 2008-12-16
I had a similar problem getting 1920x1200@60hz, I used the nvidia-settings (had to upgrade nvidia drivers to 180.16 to get it to work as it kept segfaulting when i saved to xorg.conf with it) and it added this to my screen section.. maybe you can modify it to your settings...

    ```
Option         "metamodes" "1920x1200_60 +0+0"
```

Regards

XsCode

---

### Post by civillian on 2008-12-16
how do I find out what version of the drivers I have installed?

---

### Post by XsCode on 2008-12-16
180.16 are the latest beta drivers and not available via apt I don't think... 

you can check the version with nvidia-settings

---

### Post by civillian on 2008-12-16
I'm using the 177.82 drivers (reccomended by restricted drivers manager)...how would I go about upgrading them to the beta 180.16?

---

### Post by XsCode on 2008-12-16
32bit are [here]("ftp://download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/180.16/NVIDIA-Linux-x86-180.16-pkg1.run")

64Bit are [here]("ftp://download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86_64/180.16/NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-180.16-pkg2.run")

**you may want to print this bit out...**

[LIST=1]
[*]Make sure you have build-essential installed
[*]in a terminal type 
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
```
This will drop you to the command line.
[*]log in
[*]type (if you downloaded the driver to your home folder, otherwise cd to wherever you put it) 
```
sudo sh ./NVIDIA-Linux-x86-180.16-pkg1.run
```
[*]follow the instructions
[*]when you get back to the command line type 
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start
```
[/LIST]

you should now be running on 180.16

nvidia settings will have moved to Applications -> System Tools

Regards

XsCode

I tried adding ```
Option         "metamodes" "1280x1024_60 +0+0"
``` but it crashed the xserver when I reloaded it so I had to remove it. I can get a resolution of 1152x864 but its still a bit small :(

Can anyone make any more suggestions?

### Post by XsCode on 2008-12-16
try adding this line to the monitor section in xorg.conf then restarting xorg

```

modeline "1280x1024@75" 135.0 1280 1296 1440 1688 1024 1025 1028 1066 +hsync +vsync

```

P.S. Happy Birthday for next week ;)

---

```?

### Post by XsCode on 2008-12-16
Just as it's written... like this

> 
Section "Monitor"
  Identifier "Configured Monitor"
  Vendorname "LG Electronics Inc."
  Modelname "LG L1718S"
  Horizsync 30.0-83.0
  Vertrefresh 50.0-75.0
  modeline "1280x1024@60" 108.0 1280 1328 1440 1688 1024 1025 1028 1066 +hsync +vsync
  Gamma 1.0
EndSection


Note that you shouldn't change the identifier if it's different from what i've put...

hmmmm... are you using the nvidia-settings to change the resolution or the screen resolution prog in preferences?  I used the nvidia-settings by running it as sudo from a terminal (to give it permissions to write to xorg.conf)

### Post by amdalex on 2008-12-16
I just changed my screen resolution from System> Preferences> Screen Resolution and selected 1280x1024. I also have a Geforce8400 and the 177 driver.

---

### Post by XsCode on 2008-12-16
I *think* it all depends on the monitors ability to provide valid EDID information.  I know my cheap Acer 24" doesn't and therefore i had to provide a modeline for it....

---

### Post by civillian on 2008-12-17
It all works now, so thanks for the help! But I think it was the fact that the monitor was not providing the information, and the horizontal and vertical refresh rates were incorrectky guessed by the computer, which meant that it thought it couldn't acheive a resolution higher than 1024x768. Changing it in the xorg.conf let me have a higher res!

---
